Die besonderen Akte des Geschehens verlangen zu ihrem Verständnis überall die Zurückführung auf besondere Kräfte und damit, im letzten Sinne, auf besondere Bewußtseinszentren und Einheiten 3).
en_paragraphs: the effect cannot surpass the cause, so surely the living cannot arise from the lifeless, nor draw its nourishment from it. And just as we are here driven to ascribe sense and consciousness to the individual members, so the regulated course and harmony of all the partial processes leads us to think them subordinated to a comprehensive animate whole: `there is therefore a world-soul, a single life that fills and streams through everything, holds and links everything together within itself, in order to make the machine of the whole world into one unity` (ut unam reddat totius mundi machinam) 1). Thus mechanism itself and its all-encompassing validity cannot be thought without the idea of universal animation. This idea forms, at the level of consideration on which we are here situated, no special metaphysical assumption that would be added from outside as a supplement to the matter and content of experience: it is directly the presupposition for the first articulation and formulation of the problem of nature itself. In the image of the cosmos that now stands before us, there is first expressed in symbolic shell the strict and universal demand of continuous causal lawfulness, by virtue of which every influence exerted upon a single point extends, in its consequences, to the totality of all appearances. `As in the human body the movement of one member calls forth that of another, and as on a lute, when one string is touched, all the others sound along with it, so every movement of a part of the world is felt and imitated in all the others` 2). The concept of the world-organism reached here is the first form in which the thought of the self-sufficiency of natural laws clothes itself. No change can now any longer be posited through alien arbitrariness, whether of human or `daemonic` kind, unless it is at the same time determined and prescribed by the proper conditions lying in the momentary state of things and in their inner law of development.

Even the thought of magic, which pervades and dominates Agrippa's whole view, thereby receives a new aim. We saw how, already in the Middle Ages, magic and astrology served, in opposition to religious subjectivism, to assert and reinforce the conception of nature as an objective power guided by independent laws. (Cf. above, p. 155f.) Now this conception presses further, as in place of the magic of symbols and signs there develops a `natural magic` that no longer thinks to master things by the secret power of the word, but through command over their regular inner dispositions and capacities. This transformation, which finds its expression and completion in the sixteenth century in Giambattista Porta's well-known work on natural magic 1), is present already in germ in Agrippa, though in him the older mode of representation still predominates overall. `The knowledge of the dependence of things in their succession` is, as he states in the same connection, `the foundation of every wonder-working, and it is mistaken to believe that an event exceeds nature and runs counter to it which can occur only from it and in accordance with it.` The historical course of thought does not proceed from a mechanical image of the universe to which imagination subsequently lends life and ensoulment; rather, the original concrete intuition of unity, which sets motion and life as one, is the precondition out of which the concept of mechanism is won through scientific analysis and differentiation. —

But just as here the thought of the world-soul precedes and prepares the new concept of nature, so on the other side it serves to preserve and express the irreducible peculiarity of consciousness. Consciousness cannot be derived from the things and elements of nature as a secondary result and explained from them: it must be laid into them from the outset as a first and necessary determination. `Sense` is — as Campanella declares when, as the last in the line of natural philosophers, he once more sums up the result of the whole movement — no external property, no mode adhering to any individual being and remaining restricted to it, but an essential attribute and an active force, `sensus non videtur esse modus quidem existentiae, sed res essentialis visque activa` 1). In this interpenetration of consciousness and force we see before us a metaphysical motif that continues to work far into modern philosophy and that points above all to Leibniz. The natural philosophy of the Renaissance created the material that Leibniz lays at the basis of his view and theory of the organism. Here too force is not a product of being, but its necessary condition: we cannot ascribe to any being a determinate existence without first endowing it with determinate `powers` and capacities. The existence of each thing is conditioned by the fact that it possesses the tendency to work onward from the individual point of existence into which it seems at first to be set, to spread itself out and multiply itself 2). The reality of the natural body contains within itself a multiplicity of spatial articulations, as well as a succession of temporal phases of development: we can understand both only if we relate the plurality back to a unity that unfolds and reveals itself in it. This unity in multiplicity, which we think in the concept of the indivisible qualitatively simple force as against its successive expressions,

designates at the same time the fundamental character of what we call life: `vita dicitur a vi` 1). There is no being without acting, no acting without an analogue of consciousness: all existence is thus life conscious of itself 2). Yet within this doctrine of all-unity the individual still does not disappear without remainder into the absolute, but signifies over against it a problem of its own and a new claim. It is a vain attempt to want to trace all efficacy of nature back to the divine primal essence and ground it exhaustively in it: concrete occurrence everywhere requires for its explanation peculiar and individual principles. It is not God who, as a certain metaphysical theory wills, strives upward in the flame and shines in the sun, but the proper specific nature of fire and light. The perfection of natural things proves itself precisely in this, that they possess within themselves the germ and the capacity for self-preservation. Thus in particular the human soul is freed from the necessity of supernatural assistance: it is itself, not a transcendent power, that thinks our thoughts and guides our willing and doing. The particular acts of occurrence everywhere demand for their understanding that they be traced back to particular forces, and thereby, in the last sense, to particular centers and unities of consciousness 3).

parts.title: Part 54: Potency Critique and Definition of Substance

sections.heading: `Die Kritik des Potenzbegriffs` / `Die Definition der Substanz`
sections.de:
In diesen Ausführungen Campanellas ist genau das Problem bezeichnet, das sich später zu dem metaphysischen Gegensatz des Okkasionalismus und der prästabilierten Harmonie verdichtet hat 1). Allgemein beginnt hier der Begriff der Kraft jene bestimmtere und schärfere Form anzunehmen, in welcher er später von Leibniz dem `nackten Vermögen` der Scholastiker entgegengesetzt wird. An dem Aristotelischen Begriff der `Potenz` haftet von seiner Entstehung an eine innere Zweideutigkeit. Indem Aristoteles den Begriff der absoluten Materie, den Begriff einer Materie ohne jegliche Formbestimmtheit konzipiert, hat er damit einen reinen Grenzbegriff des Denkens in eine metaphysische Wesensbestimmung verwandelt. Ein Gedanke, der lediglich einer logischen Verneinung entstammt, wird zu einem realen Faktor des Werdens und Naturgeschehens. Die Scholastik versucht vergeblich in diesem Widerstreit eine Ausgleichung zu finden: sie vermag nur zwischen den beiden Gesichtspunkten, die durch ihn bezeichnet sind, abzuwechseln. Die Potenz erscheint ihr daher bald als das Mögliche im Sinne eines bloß abstrakten Beziehungsbegriffs, bald als ein eigenes selbständiges Etwas, das von sich selbst der Verwirklichung entgegenstrebt. Der Streit der Alexandristen und Averroisten hatte sich wesentlich auf diese Frage bezogen: ist die Materie bloße `Possibilität`, ist sie lediglich

die passive Grundlage der Veränderung, die alle Gestaltung von den Formen erwartet, die an sie herantreten oder besitzt sie eine eigene und selbständige Wirksamkeit? Jean de Jandun, ein berühmter Aristoteliker des 15. Jahrhunderts, hatte sich in dieser Frage bereits, entgegen der Averroistischen Grundansicht, die er im allgemeinen vertritt, für das Letztere entschieden: die Potenz bedeutet, wie er ausdrücklich betont, keine bloße Beziehung, die unser Denken in die Materie hineinlegt, sondern sie ist als ein reales Etwas in ihr vorhanden und wirksam 1). An diese Entwicklung, die sich innerhalb der Aristotelischen Schule selbst vollzogen hatte, knüpft nunmehr die Naturphilosophie der Renaissance überall an. Schon bei Cardano wird hervorgehoben, daß die Materie nicht lediglich als ein Bestimmungsloses, sondern als eine eigene Weise der Bestimmtheit zu fassen sei, und daß ihr somit eine durchaus positive `Wirklichkeit` zukomme 2). Telesio und Patrizzi verschärfen diesen Gedanken, indem sie eine neue Darlegung der Beziehung von Form und Materie suchen, die sich außerhalb der traditionellen Begriffsgegensätze hält. Die Art, wie die Dinge in ihrem `Samen` enthalten sind, bildet — wie hier ausgeführt wird — ein fundamentales sachliches Grundverhältnis, das durch die herkömmliche Unterscheidung von Akt und Potenz eher verhüllt, als verdeutlicht wird. Denn der gegenwärtige Zustand besitzt,

sofern er als Keim und schöpferische Bedingung künftiger Zustände und Veränderungen gedacht wird, zugleich das höchste `aktuelle` Sein; — er ist `wirklich`, weil er ein eigentümlicher und notwendiger Faktor im Prozeß des Wirkens selbst ist. Eine andere Art des Seins aber kann es nicht geben: die Naturbetrachtung kennt nur solche `Wesenheiten`, die sich in tatsächlichen Kräften und Tätigkeiten äußern. Die Annahme eines Vermögens, das nicht in sich selbst das Streben zu seiner Verwirklichung trägt, sondern als gleichgültiges Substrat alle Bestimmung von außen erwartet, ist ein haltloses logisches Zwitterding. Sie macht eine subjektive gedankliche Vergleichung, die wir zwischen dem gegenwärtigen und künftigen Zustand anstellen, zu einem eigenen und selbständigen Sein. So beruht — wie jetzt im Einzelnen gezeigt wird — die Zweiteilung, die die gesamte Peripatetische Naturlehre beherrscht, auf einer ontologischen Verwechslung. Die empirische Betrachtung der Natur bietet zu einer derartigen Verdoppelung der Prinzipien keinerlei Anlaß. Sie sieht in den räumlich und zeitlich ausgebreiteten Dingen nur die extensive Gestalt und Erscheinungsform derselben Kräfte, die intensiv bereits im `Samen` eingeschlossen sind und seine eigentliche Realität ausmachen 1).

Damit aber sind wir zu einer eingreifenden Kritik des Substanzbegriffs überhaupt gelangt, die nunmehr von Patrizzi im Einzelnen durchgeführt wird. `Man eröffne uns endlich jenes Allerheiligste, jenes Geheimnis aller Geheimnisse, von dem beständig die Rede ist, ohne daß es uns jemals vor Augen gestellt wird. Was ist die Substanz? Nach der Sache, nicht nach dem Namen frage ich: um zu erfahren, ob die Form in Wirklichkeit, nicht nur dem Namen nach, die Substanz bildet. Man antwortet mir zunächst, Substanz sei das, was dem Dinge das Sein gibt. Gibt aber — so frage ich weiter — nur die Form dem Dinge das Sein oder auch die Materie? Beides, entgegnet man, nur gibt die Materie lediglich ein potentielles, die Form dagegen das aktuelle Sein. Aber hier entsteht ein neuer Zweifel: genügen nicht die bestimmten Qualitäten des Warmen und Kalten, des Feuchten und Trocknen, des Schweren und Leichten, um die besonderen Gestaltungen der Materie zu erklären? Sind es also nicht diese Qualitäten, die den besonderen Körpern ihre spezifische Seinsart geben? Man zaudert lange: endlich aber erwidert man, die Form sei das, was das Wesentliche in einer Sache bilde und woraus ihre eigentümlichen Betätigungsweisen entsprängen. Aber ist dasjenige, was z. B. dem Feuer wesentlich: ist nicht gerade die Wärme und die sonstigen, mit ihr zusammenhängenden Eigenschaften, da doch alle Tätigkeiten des Feuers, das Wärmen, das Dörren, das Verdünnen u. s. f. lediglich von ihr herstammen? So bildet also die Wärme die Form des Feuers, die sich damit in eine Summe von `Accidentien` auflöst. Wenn die Aristoteliker uns alle diese Schwierigkeiten lösen, so wollen wir uns gern vor der Subtilität ihrer Philosophie beugen; lösen sie sie nicht, so mögen sie die Richtigkeit unseres Schlusses zugestehen. Man gebe uns endlich nach so viel Jahrhunderten eine allgemeingültige Definition der Substanz, oder man höre auf in der Philosophie jene einförmige alte Weise zu wiederholen: die Form ist Substanz, weil sie das Wesentliche in der Sache bildet, weil sie Tätigkeiten, die der
sections.en:
In these expositions of Campanella, the very problem that later condensed into the metaphysical opposition of occasionalism and pre-established harmony is already precisely marked out 1). In general, the concept of force here begins to assume that more determinate and sharper form in which it is later set by Leibniz against the `bare faculty` of the scholastics. An inner ambiguity clings to the Aristotelian concept of `potency` from its very origin. In conceiving the concept of absolute matter, the concept of a matter without any determination of form whatever, Aristotle thereby transformed a pure boundary concept of thought into a metaphysical determination of essence. A thought springing merely from a logical negation becomes a real factor of becoming and of natural events. Scholasticism attempts in vain to find a reconciliation in this conflict: it can do no more than alternate between the two points of view designated by it. Potency therefore appears to it now as the possible in the sense of a merely abstract relational concept, now as a self-subsistent something of its own that of itself strives toward realization. The dispute of the Alexandrists and Averroists had centered essentially on this question: is matter bare `possibility`, is it merely

the passive ground of change, awaiting all shaping from the forms that approach it, or does it possess an efficacy of its own, independent and self-subsistent? Jean de Jandun, a famous Aristotelian of the fifteenth century, had in this question already decided for the latter, contrary to the Averroist basic view he generally upholds: potency, as he expressly emphasizes, signifies no mere relation that our thought lays into matter, but is present and effective in it as a real something 1). To this development, which had taken place within the Aristotelian school itself, Renaissance natural philosophy now everywhere attaches itself. Already in Cardano it is emphasized that matter is to be grasped not merely as something indeterminate, but as a mode of determinacy of its own, and that a thoroughly positive `reality` therefore belongs to it 2). Telesio and Patrizzi sharpen this thought by seeking a new exposition of the relation of form and matter that remains outside the traditional conceptual oppositions. The way in which things are contained in their `seed` constitutes, as is explained here, a fundamental objective basic relation, one that the customary distinction between act and potency conceals rather than clarifies. For the present state possesses,

insofar as it is thought as germ and creative condition of future states and changes, at the same time the highest `actual` being; it is `actual` because it is a peculiar and necessary factor in the process of efficacy itself. But there can be no other kind of being: the contemplation of nature knows only such `essences` as express themselves in actual forces and activities. The assumption of a faculty that does not carry within itself the striving toward its realization, but as an indifferent substrate awaits all determination from without, is an untenable logical hybrid. It makes a subjective comparison of thought, which we institute between the present and the future state, into a being of its own and self-subsistent. Thus the bifurcation that governs the whole Peripatetic doctrine of nature rests, as is now shown in detail, upon an ontological confusion. The empirical consideration of nature affords no occasion whatever for such a doubling of principles. In the things extended in space and time it sees only the extensive shape and phenomenal form of the same forces that are already intensively enclosed in the `seed` and constitute its proper reality 1).

With this, however, we have arrived at a far-reaching critique of the concept of substance as such, now carried out in detail by Patrizzi. `Let them finally open to us that holy of holies, that mystery of all mysteries, of which there is constant talk without ever being set before our eyes. What is substance? I ask after the thing, not the name, in order to learn whether form in reality, and not merely in name, constitutes substance. One first answers me that substance is what gives being to the thing. But, I ask further, does only form give being to the thing, or does matter as well? Both, comes the answer, only matter gives merely a potential being, form actual being. But here a new doubt arises: are not the determinate qualities of the warm and the cold, the moist and the dry, the heavy and the light sufficient to explain the particular configurations of matter? Are these qualities not therefore what give particular bodies their specific mode of being? One hesitates long; at last one replies that form is what constitutes the essential in a thing and from which its peculiar modes of activity spring. But is not precisely what is essential, for example, to fire heat and the other properties connected with it, since all the activities of fire, warming, drying, rarefying, and so forth, spring solely from it? Thus heat forms the "form" of fire, which thereby dissolves into a sum of `accidents`. If the Aristotelians solve all these difficulties for us, we will gladly bow before the subtlety of their philosophy; if they do not, then let them concede the correctness of our conclusion. Let someone finally give us, after so many centuries, a universally valid definition of substance, or let people stop repeating in philosophy that uniform old manner: form is substance, because it constitutes the essential in the thing, because it gives rise to activities, which the

parts.title: Part 55: Immanent Purpose and Paracelsus Opening

sections.heading: `Die Bewegung und der ,,unbewegte Beweger“` / `Paracelsus`
sections.de:
Sache eigentümlich sind, hervorbringt, weil sie dem Dinge das Sein gibt, weil sie das τί ἦν εἶναι, das ,,Was“, den Vernunftbegriff, die Definition des Gegenstandes enthält.`1) Denn alle diese Erklärungen lassen sich ebensowohl auf die empirisch-phänomenalen Eigenschaften der Objekte beziehen, die sonach dasjenige ausmachen würden, was wir als ihr wahrhaftes Sein zu bezeichnen haben`1).

Einen neuen Ausdruck findet diese Grundanschauung in der Erörterung und Kritik des Aristotelischen Zweckbegriffs. Sosehr die Naturansicht des Aristoteles verlangt, daß die ,,Formen“ der Dinge als die immanenten Zwecke gefaßt werden, denen sie zustreben, so wird doch diese Einsicht durch den Abschluß, den seine Metaphysik erhält, wiederum in Frage gestellt. Denn als der letzte Grund und als das Endziel alles Geschehens erscheint hier der ,,unbewegte Beweger“, der außerhalb der Welt und von ihrem Sein und Werden unberührt, ein unabhängiges Dasein führt. Der Zweck der Entwicklung liegt somit nicht mehr in der Selbstverwirklichung der inneren Wesenheit der Dinge, sondern in einem jenseitigen Sein: der Weltbegriff hat seine Selbständigkeit an den Gottesbegriff verloren. Dieser innere Widerstreit des Systems ist es, an den die Polemik und die Reform des Telesio anknüpft. Wenn alle Wandlung der Wesen in ihnen selbst nicht zum Abschluß kommt, wenn ein äußeres Ziel ihr Weg und Richtung weist, so schwindet unter diesem Gesichtspunkt der Wert und die Selbständigkeit der Einzelwesen dahin. Die Formen, sofern sie individuelle Prinzipien des Wachstums und der Entwicklung bedeuten sollen, werden zu müßigen Erdichtungen: all ihre Wirksamkeit löst sich in die des Einen allumfassenden Urwesens auf. Wenn die Naturdinge in sich selbst zuletzt keine bewegende Kraft und keinen Antrieb zur Veränderung besitzen, wenn all ihre scheinbare Tätigkeit in Wahrheit somit nur ein Leiden ist, so fehlt uns jedes Mittel, sie voneinander zu unterscheiden und als gesonderte Substanzen zu behaupten`1).

Nur die Immanenz des Zweckes vermag dem Sein seine Unabhängigkeit, wie seine Fülle und gegliederte Mannigfaltigkeit zurückzugeben. Jede Gattung ist um ihrer selbst willen geschaffen und besitzt in sich den Mittelpunkt ihres Daseins und Wirkens; wenngleich alle in stetiger Stufenfolge miteinander verbunden sind und aufeinander hinweisen. Von den Metallen zu den Pflanzen, von diesen zu den niederen Tieren und den Fischen und Vögeln, höher hinauf zu den Säugetieren und Menschen führt eine beständig fortschreitende körperliche Organisation, der wir eine entsprechende, immer reichere Entfaltung in den Graden des Bewußtseins zugeordnet denken müssen`2).` Der Begriff des Weltorganismus verbietet, irgendeine einzelne Art schlechthin als Mittel für die Ziele einer höheren zu denken: er fordert, daß wir in jedem, noch so begrenzten Sein das Gesamtgesetz vollständig verkörpert finden. An Stelle der äußerlichen Betrachtung des Zweckes tritt die Erkenntnis der durchgehenden Harmonie im Bau und in der Entwicklung aller Lebewesen. So können wir von der Verwandlung einzelner Pflanzenarten in einander auf den gleichen Prozeß im gesamten Bereich des Organischen schließen; so verstattet uns bestimmte ontogenetische Vorgänge, wie die Entwicklung der Raupe zum Schmetterling, einen Einblick in die prinzipielle Möglichkeit der Umbildung der tierischen Spezies. Solche Gedanken, die sich z. B. bei Vanini finden`1),` treten freilich noch zusammenhanglos und untermischt mit abenteuerlichen Analogien und Spekulationen auf; aber sie deuten dennoch auf eine umfassende geistige Bewegung zurück. Dem Naturbegriff erwächst, verglichen mit der mittelalterlichen Anschauung, ein tieferer Sinn. Nicht unmittelbar als Abbild der Gottheit, sondern nach dem Muster der Welt, also als ,,Bild des Bildes“ ist -- wie Agrippa von Nettesheim ausspricht -- der Mensch geschaffen: nicht direkt, sondern nur durch die Vermittlung der gesamten organischen Wirklichkeit vermag er sich somit zu erkennen und seine Beziehung zum ,,Absoluten“ zu erfassen`2).` Schon die ausgehende Scholastik hatte in Raimund von Sabonde auf diesen Gedanken hingedeutet, der sodann in der Schule des Nikolaus Cusanus durchgebildet und weitergeführt wurde: jetzt wächst er im Natursystem des Paracelsus zu zentraler Bedeutung heran.

Die Grundansicht des Paracelsus von der wechselseitigen Entsprechung des Mikrokosmos und Makrokosmos setzt ein neues Verhältnis des Geistigen und Natürlichen voraus. Die isolierte Betrachtung des Menschen muß notwendig ins Dunkel und in die Irre führen; alles Licht, das wir über sein Wesen zu gewinnen vermögen, strahlt lediglich von der Erkenntnis des einheitlichen Lebens der Gesamtnatur zurück. ,,Also ist der Mensch ein bildtnuss in eim spiegel gesetzt hinein durch die vier Element ... Darumb so ist die Philosophey nichts anders, allein das gantz wissen und erkanntnuß des dings, das den glantz im spiegel gibt. Und zu gleicher weiß wie der im Spiegel niemandts mag seins wesens verstand geben, niemandts zuerkennen geben, was er sey, dann allein es steht da, wie eine todte bildtnuss: Also ist der Mensch an ihm selbst auch und auss ime wird nichts genommen, allein was aus der eußern erkanntnuß kompt, des figur er im spiegel ist"`1).` Es gilt den Mikrokosmos durch die ,,Eltern des Mikrokosmos“, die Art und das Individuum aus dem Gesamtgesetz, das sie in sich verkörpern und reflektieren, zu begreifen. Damit schwindet der dualistische Gegensatz, der bisher die ,,himmlische“ und die ,,irdische“ Welt getrennt hatte. Ein und dieselbe Regel ist es, die uns die ,,niedere“ und die ,,höhere“ Sphäre, die uns den Leib des Menschen und das Firmament erkennen lehrt und die uns damit zur Einsicht bringt, ,,wie ein Firmament, ein Gestirn, ein natur und ein wesen da sey under getheilter gstalt und form"`2).` Wieder ist es der Gedanke des Weltorganismus, der zum Vehikel für den Einheitsbegriff der Natur und Erfahrung wird. Und es ist interessant und merkwürdig, wie dieser metaphysische Gedanke bei Paracelsus sich allmählich mit empirischem Gehalt erfüllt und wie er bis in die besondere Gestaltung seiner Arzneikunde hinein sich fruchtbar erweist. Die Medizin kann sich nur auf dem Grunde der allgemeinen theoretischen Naturbetrachtung erheben; das ,,Umwenden“ der großen Welt in die kleine gibt den Arzt. Was in dem einzelnen Teile unsichtbar und unbegreiflich liegt, das wird in dieser Rückbeziehung auf das Ganze sichtbar und greiflich: der äußere Himmel wird der Wegweiser des inneren`3).` Zugleich aber fordert die Grundanschauung, die wir von der Wirkungsweise der organischen Natur gewonnen haben, daß wir alle Vorgänge, die sich im Körper abspielen, nicht lediglich als
sections.en:
... because it gives being to the thing, because it contains the τί ἦν εἶναι, the "what," the rational concept, the definition of the object.' For all these explanations can equally well be referred to the empirical-phenomenal properties of objects, which would then make up that which we have to designate as their true being`1).

A new expression of this basic view is found in the discussion and critique of the Aristotelian concept of purpose. However much Aristotle's view of nature demands that the "forms" of things be grasped as the immanent ends toward which they strive, this insight is nevertheless called into question again by the way his metaphysics is completed. For here the "unmoved mover" appears as the ultimate ground and the final end of all that happens, leading an independent existence outside the world and untouched by its being and becoming. The end of development thus no longer lies in the self-realization of the inner essence of things, but in a transcendent being: the concept of the world has lost its self-sufficiency to the concept of God. It is to this inner conflict of the system that Telesio's polemic and reform attach themselves. If all transformation of beings does not come to completion within themselves, if an external goal points out their way and direction, then from this point of view the worth and independence of individual beings vanish. The forms, insofar as they are meant to signify individual principles of growth and development, become idle fictions: all their efficacy dissolves into that of the one all-encompassing primordial being. If the natural things themselves ultimately possess no moving force and no impulse to change, if all their seeming activity is therefore in truth mere passivity, then we lack every means of distinguishing them from one another and asserting them as separate substances`1).

Only the immanence of purpose can restore to being both its independence and its fullness and articulated multiplicity. Each genus is created for its own sake and possesses within itself the center of its existence and efficacy, although all are connected with one another in a steady sequence of stages and point toward one another. From metals to plants, from these to the lower animals and the fishes and birds, higher still to the mammals and human beings, there leads a steadily advancing bodily organization, to which we must think correspondingly assigned an ever richer unfolding in the grades of consciousness`2).` The concept of the world-organism forbids thinking any individual species simply as a means for the ends of a higher one: it demands that in every being, however limited, we find the total law fully embodied. In place of the external consideration of purpose steps the recognition of the thoroughgoing harmony in the structure and development of all living beings. Thus from the transformation of individual plant species into one another we can infer the same process in the whole domain of the organic; thus certain ontogenetic processes, such as the development of the caterpillar into the butterfly, permit us an insight into the principled possibility of the transformation of animal species. Such thoughts, as are found for example in Vanini`1),` still appear, to be sure, disconnected and intermixed with adventurous analogies and speculations; yet they nevertheless point back to a comprehensive spiritual movement. Compared with the medieval view, the concept of nature acquires a deeper meaning. Man is created not immediately as an image of the Godhead, but after the pattern of the world, thus as an "image of the image," as Agrippa von Nettesheim says; only indirectly, therefore, only through the mediation of the whole organic reality, can he come to know himself and grasp his relation to the "absolute"`2).` Already the late scholastic period had pointed toward this thought in Raimund of Sabunde, and it was then worked out and carried further in the school of Nicholas of Cusa: now it grows in Paracelsus's system of nature into central significance.

Paracelsus's basic view of the reciprocal correspondence of microcosm and macrocosm presupposes a new relation between the spiritual and the natural. The isolated consideration of man must necessarily lead into darkness and error; all the light we can gain about his nature shines back solely from the knowledge of the unified life of nature as a whole. "Thus man is an image set into a mirror through the four elements ... Therefore philosophy is nothing other than the whole knowledge and cognition of the thing that gives the gleam in the mirror. And just as the one in the mirror can give no one understanding of his essence, can make no one know what he is, but only stands there like a dead image: so too the human being in himself, and nothing is taken from out of him; only what comes from outer cognition, of that he is the figure in the mirror"`1).` It is necessary to understand the microcosm through the "parents of the microcosm" -- species and individual -- from the total law that they embody and reflect within themselves. Thereby the dualistic opposition that had hitherto separated the "heavenly" and the "earthly" world vanishes. It is one and the same rule that teaches us to know the "lower" and the "higher" sphere, that teaches us to know the body of man and the firmament, and that thereby brings us to insight into "how there is one firmament, one constellation, one nature and one essence there under divided shape and form"`2).` Once again it is the thought of the world-organism that becomes the vehicle for the unitary concept of nature and experience. And it is interesting and remarkable how this metaphysical thought in Paracelsus gradually fills itself with empirical content, and how it proves fruitful even down into the particular formation of his medicine. Medicine can rise only on the ground of general theoretical contemplation of nature; the "turning over" of the great world into the small gives the physician. What lies in the individual part invisible and incomprehensible becomes, in this relation-back to the whole, visible and tangible: the outer heaven becomes the guide to the inner`3).` At the same time, however, the basic view we have gained of the mode of operation of organic nature demands that we regard all processes that take place in the body not merely as

glossary.notes: The title term is broader and more historical than ordinary academic `epistemology`.
Needs contextual discipline. `Knowledge` is the default, but some passages may require a more active rendering.
Keep pressure on the Kantian background without overloading every occurrence.

Use for load-bearing logical units rather than for any merely important notion. In the introduction Cassirer treats `Grundbegriffe` as the conceptual means by which science orders experience, and later chapters reactivate the pressure when historical epochs reformulate the basic conditions of knowing.
Do not vary casually. Cassirer often builds arguments by repetition and recombination.
A bookkeeping and calculation image for concepts reduced to provisional devices. `Counters` is too flat, while `tokens` now carries avoidable computational baggage.
Preserve the philosophical term without flattening it into generic `prior assumption`. In adjectival contexts, prefer `a priori` or `aprioristic` according to the sentence.
Keep the Latin when Cassirer is explicitly naming Cusa's formula or book-title. In expository paraphrase, `learned ignorance` is the current front-runner.
A technical Cusan term, not just a casual guess. Cassirer sometimes paraphrases it as `Annahme`, but the Latin should stay visible where the term itself is doing philosophical work.
In the Cusa chapter this names the older, more passive model of cognition as resemblance to what is already there.
The key Cusan reversal. Cassirer uses it for the active process by which mind establishes the relation to its objects and, in mathematics, measures them from forms it finds in itself.
A central Cusan term. In Cassirer it names the simple or implicit mode in which multiplicity is contained before development.
The counterpart to `complicatio`: the developed articulation of what was previously enfolded.

The classic Cusan formula standing behind Cassirer's German `Coincidenz der Gegensätze`. Keep the Latin visible where the technical doctrine, not just a generic paradox, is at stake. Distinguish two pressures: an early mystical/theological sense in which oppositions are resolved in the divine infinite, and the current Cassirerian methodological/logical sense in which apparently separated domains are linked through a productive rule or continuous transition.

A major pressure point in the Cusa chapter. Cassirer locally aligns it with `Verstand` and discursive, abstractive cognition governed by the excluded middle; do not settle the English globally too early.

In the Aristotelian psychology section this renders the `nous pathetikos`: the understanding insofar as it receives and gathers the material offered by sense and imagination. Technical context may favor `intellect` over literal `understanding`.

In the Aristotelian psychology section this names the power treated as independent of sensible conditioning and later radicalized in Averroism. Keep open whether the historical register is better served by `active intellect` or `agent intellect`.
In the current tranche this is the higher faculty that comprehends principle and totality in a single act. The phrase `visus intellectualis` names its operative mode.
In the current Cusa tranche Cassirer rereads the Neoplatonic term methodologically: not merely a cosmological descent, but the logical demand to derive the particular from an originating ground.

Cusa's late title-formula. Cassirer treats it as holding together two claims at once: the absolute as immanent to finite things, and the absolute as not determinable like any individual thing. Keep the Latin visible where the treatise-title or technical doctrine is in view.

In the current Cusa tranche this names the presupposed unity of all knowing. Cassirer uses it to mark the shift from an ontological grounding in absolute being toward an inner certainty native to inquiry itself.

In the Bovillus section this names the goal of a true logic capable of presenting opposed moments, their relation, and their eventual coincidence. Keep the Latin visible when Cassirer is naming Bovillus's formula or work-title.
Cassirer treats this as only touched by Cusa but first given determinate Renaissance form in Bovillus, before it reappears more forcefully in Paracelsus.

A scholastic technical term left partially Latinized in English. In the Bovillus tranche it carries a deliberate doubleness: both the formal content of the thing and the sensible or conceptual image received from it.

In the Aristotelian psychology section this names the image-content on whose ground thought, as a psychological act, must still arise. `Phantasm` preserves the scholastic-Aristotelian register, but can sound too technical if overused.

In the current Bovillus conclusion Cassirer names the contradiction within the Aristotelian `Substanzbegriff`: the individual thing as true substance, yet also an inner substantial essence reached only through universal form.
The opening thesis of the Humanism chapter. Cassirer treats the Renaissance as united above all by a struggle against the scholastic doctrine of the `substantial form`.
Explicitly paired with `Substanzbegriff` in the Humanism chapter: modern physics first accomplishes the step from being to activity by moving from a substance-concept to a function-concept.
In the Platonism section this names the Greek speculative content that Christianity had absorbed and that Renaissance Platonism seeks to restore beyond merely theological use.
The Platonic doctrine of recollection. In the Ficino tranche Cassirer treats its transmission as a fixed historical center for the modern development of the concept of consciousness.
Cassirer is still using the Latinized form in the Cusa chapter; `precision` is the current default, but the term may later need differentiation from ordinary exactness.
Keep the Latin visible where Cusa explicitly contrasts `quidditas` with `quantitas`. `Whatness` is philosophically pointed, but can sound too scholastic if overused.
Usually `quantity`, but preserve the Latin in the explicit Cusan contrast with `quidditas`.
The Ficino tranche makes this structurally important: a non-compositional determination such as light or color, present whole in each part and mediating between body and soul.
In the current Ficino tranche this names the order of divisible extension and composition. Cassirer isolates the distinction between `Quantität` and `Qualität` as the passage's pure logical core.

A major pressure point. English `science` is often too narrow, but `knowledge` is too broad. In the introduction Cassirer often means the rational or scientific disciplines that furnish critique with its current foothold, while other contexts widen toward systematic knowledge or learned inquiry.

Keep open where Cassirer is explicitly distinguishing historical, psychological, and scientific layers. In the introduction it is not mere raw givenness, but the field whose content is selected, organized, and repeatedly redefined by scientific work.

Likely to be singular or plural by context; preserve the historical phrase where possible. In the introduction this names the domain, above all mathematical natural science, in which the unity of the concept of knowledge first gains steady proof and historical efficacy.
Preserve the explicit methodological claim rather than smoothing it into generic critical philosophy.

Use contextually. The calibration tranche pressures both `mind` and `spirit`; do not settle the term globally too early. The introduction now leans toward `mind`, `intellectual`, or `conceptual` where Cassirer rejects `Geist` as a finished substance or names the non-religious sphere of thought and culture.
Needs contextual care. `Human sciences` is the current front-runner in the introduction, but the term remains bound to the unresolved pressure on `Geist`.
The Kantian background matters. In the introduction prefer `representation` where Cassirer is measuring truth against `Vorstellungen`; `idea` is often too loose for those epistemic contexts.
Prefer `self-consciousness` in methodological or historical-philosophical passages unless the psychological sense dominates.

In the Zabarella tranche Cassirer explicitly renders the modern stake of this term as the deductive movement from causes or presuppositions toward effects. Keep the historical term visible where it is paired with `resolutive Methode`.

Load-bearing in the Zabarella tranches. Distinguish it from mathematical `analysis`: in the strict sense it names the natural-scientific path from effects toward the discovery of hidden causes, with `inventio` rather than completed `scientia` as its immediate goal.

Cassirer's key bridge from Zabarella to Galileo. Not mere accumulation of cases, but induction exercised on `necessary matter` and essential connection, such that a universal law is seen in and through particular examples.

Nizolius's counter-term to scholastic abstraction. The universal concept does not isolate a hidden common nature, but contracts the verified judgments about individuals into a concise linguistic and logical expression.

Keep the historical spelling where Cassirer is following Reuchlin's Renaissance universal-tradition model. In the current section it names the unitary stream into which Greek and Hebrew wisdom are made to converge.

Load-bearing in the history-philosophy tranche. Cassirer uses the Latin formula not as a loose slogan, but as the ordering principle under which a universal history of philosophy is first constructed; keep the Latin visible where the Renaissance self-description itself is in view.
